title: Stack and Monotonic Stack
description: java.util.Stack vs the recommended ArrayDeque-as-stack from Queue and Deque, plus the monotonic stack pattern for next-greater-element problems.
objectives: Use ArrayDeque as a stack, and know why it's preferred over java.util.Stack
Solve balanced parentheses (recap) and next greater element with a stack
Understand the monotonic stack pattern and why it's O(n)

sections.text: 1. Two Ways to Get a Stack in Java
In Queue and Deque, we previewed using ArrayDeque with push()/pop() for LIFO (stack) behavior. Java also has an older class, java.util.Stack -- but it's now considered legacy.

java.util.Stack extends Vector, an older, synchronized (thread-safety-overhead) class from early Java -- it works, but is not recommended for new code. ArrayDeque is faster and is Java's officially recommended stack implementation today.

We already solved this exact problem in Queue and Deque -- push every opening bracket, pop on a matching close, and check the stack is empty at the end. It's worth recognizing this is fundamentally a STACK problem: the most RECENT unmatched opener must be matched first, which is exactly LIFO order.
3. Next Greater Element: A New Stack Pattern

For each element, find the next element to its right that's GREATER than it. Brute force checks every pair -- O(n^2), like the Big-O Notation hasDuplicate example. A monotonic stack solves it in O(n).

A stack maintained so its elements are always in increasing (or decreasing) order -- new elements pop off anything on top that violates this order before being pushed, often computing something useful during each pop.

sections.title: Tracing nextGreaterElement({2,1,3,4,2})
steps: i=0 (val 2): stack empty -- push index 0. stack=[0]
i=1 (val 1): nums[1]=1 > nums[stack.peek()=0]=2? No -- push index 1. stack=[0,1]
i=2 (val 3): nums[2]=3 > nums[1]=1? Yes -- pop 1, result[1]=3. Now nums[2]=3 > nums[0]=2? Yes -- pop 0, result[0]=3. stack empty -- push index 2. stack=[2]
i=3 (val 4): nums[3]=4 > nums[2]=3? Yes -- pop 2, result[2]=4. stack empty -- push index 3. stack=[3]
i=4 (val 2): nums[4]=2 > nums[3]=4? No -- push index 4. stack=[3,4]
Loop ends. Indices 3 and 4 never found a greater element to their right -- their result stays -1

sections.title: Why this is O(n), not O(n^2)
points: Each index is pushed onto the stack EXACTLY ONCE
Each index is popped AT MOST ONCE
Total push+pop operations across the whole run: at most 2n -- O(n) overall, despite the nested while loop appearing to suggest otherwise

sections.text: The stack holds INDICES, not values -- this lets us write the answer directly into result[index] at the moment we pop, and still compare using nums[stack.peek()] for the actual value at that position.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using java.util.Stack in new code instead of the recommended ArrayDeque -- it still works, but isn't the modern preferred choice.
Storing VALUES on the monotonic stack instead of INDICES, losing the ability to write results back into the correct array position.
Assuming the nested while loop makes next-greater-element O(n^2) -- each element is pushed and popped at most once total, giving O(n) overall, not O(n) per outer iteration.
Forgetting to initialize the result array to a sentinel value (like -1) for elements that never find a next greater element.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: Why is ArrayDeque preferred over java.util.Stack for stack behavior in modern Java?
questions.answer: java.util.Stack extends the older, synchronized Vector class, carrying unnecessary thread-safety overhead for typical use. ArrayDeque is faster and is Java's officially recommended general-purpose stack implementation today.

questions.question: What is a monotonic stack?
questions.answer: A stack maintained so its elements stay in increasing or decreasing order -- when a new element would violate that order, elements are popped off (often computing a useful result during each pop) before the new one is pushed.

questions.question: Why is the next-greater-element algorithm O(n) despite having a nested while loop inside a for loop?
questions.answer: Each index is pushed onto the stack exactly once and popped at most once across the entire algorithm, so the total number of push/pop operations is bounded by O(n), not O(n) per outer iteration.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: ArrayDeque is Java's modern recommended stack implementation via push()/pop()/peek(), preferred over the legacy, Vector-based java.util.Stack. A monotonic stack maintains its elements in increasing or decreasing order, popping off violating elements (often computing a result during each pop) before pushing the new one. The classic example is next-greater-element: storing indices on the stack, popping whenever the current value is greater than the value at the stack's top index, and recording that as the popped index's answer. Despite a nested while loop, each index is pushed and popped at most once total, giving O(n) overall rather than O(n^2).

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Monotonic stack = a lineup of bouncers, shortest to tallest (or vice versa) -- when someone taller shows up, they KNOCK OUT everyone shorter below them first (that's their 'next greater'), before taking their own spot in line.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: Why is ArrayDeque preferred over java.util.Stack?
What does 'monotonic' mean in the context of a monotonic stack?
Why does next-greater-element run in O(n) despite its nested while loop?
Why does the stack store indices rather than values in this pattern?
